data.title: AVIV: Should Keep Rising If The Ceasefire Holds
data.description:
AVIV Overview

The Avantis International Large Cap Value ETF (AVIV) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund with a NAV around $1.2Bn that invests in large-capitalization equity securities outside the U.S. AVIV is a direct equity fund—it holds individual stocks, not other ETFs. The fund's benchmark target is the MSCI World ex USA Value Index, which covers large- and mid-cap value-style companies across 22 developed markets outside the U.S.

The value investment style characteristics for index construction are with three variables: book value to price, 12-month forward earnings to price, and dividend yield. While the current geopolitical shock has dragged the fund off its 52-week high of $80.80, the fund is trading in line with its peers as the Iran conflict roils European and Asian markets. For investors who believe the ceasefire holds, I think AVIV is a buy here.

Fund Breakdown

AVIV was launched by Avantis (part of American Century Investments) with a mandate to achieve broad diversification by investing in a wide set of large-cap companies across non-U.S. developed countries, specifically emphasizing key valuation signals. The fund pursues the same core philosophy Avantis applies across all its offerings—the benefits of indexing (diversification, low turnover, transparency) combined with daily oversight to tilt toward securities with higher expected returns using information in current prices. As noted in the fund's fact sheet, the portfolio management team is led by Eduardo Repetto, Ph.D., with support from Ted Randall, Daniel Ong, CFA, Mitchell Firestein, and Matthew Dubin—they are all veterans from Dimensional Fund Advisors, which gives me reasonable confidence in the pedigree of the process.

The expense ratio of 0.25% is solid for an active fund. The closest passive alternative—the iShares MSCI EAFE Value ETF (EFV) and iShares MSCI Intl Value Factor ETF (IVLU)—charges 0.30% for strictly passive exposure to a similar universe. You're getting active management, a broad portfolio of 584 holdings, and a clean value tilt for less. That's not nothing. From a liquidity standpoint, the fund trades ~89,000 shares per day on average, worth over $6MM—decent for a retail investor, though not deep enough for large institutional block trades. The fund has done well since the start of the year, and with rumors of a ceasefire holding, the fund has started to climb back.

The War Trade and Why It's a Buying Opportunity

Since the U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran intensified in early March 2026 and the Strait of Hormuz was effectively shut to tanker traffic, international equity funds like AVIV have been punished. The fund's mandate is entirely outside the U.S., with heavy exposure to developed markets in Europe and Japan—economies deeply reliant on Gulf oil flows to power their industrial bases. But with the ceasefire announcement, the case to buy AVIV at these levels is compelling. European and Asian equities have been materially oversold on energy fears, and a reopening of Hormuz should unlock a meaningful re-rating across the industrial and energy-heavy developed market names that make up the bulk of this portfolio.

Fund Portfolio and Risk Profile

The nuance that I think the market has overlooked is that AVIV's sector composition actually sets it up well even if elevated oil prices linger for another quarter. Looking at the portfolio, AVIV is significantly overweight energy at 15% versus the benchmark's 5.7%. The fund is pretty similar elsewhere and has taken a point or two off the other sectors to hedge with oil stocks, which are certain to continue to do well even with a temporary ceasefire. The strait won't just reopen to 150 tankers a day like in February, and oil should remain above $80 for the foreseeable future. Otherwise, I like this value setup.

Risks

From a risk perspective, the fund has a beta of 0.93 on a 5-year monthly basis, meaning it tracks the broader market a bit lower from a volatility standpoint. That said, with only about 4.5 years of live data since inception, I'd interpret this number with some caution—the fund has lived through a single rate cycle and a modest amount of geopolitical stress, and the true volatility profile over a full market cycle remains untested. What I can say is that the portfolio's tilt toward cash-flow-generative industrial and energy names, with a value screen applied on top, gives the fund a qualitatively defensive underpinning versus a growth-heavy benchmark. The profits to book of 0.41 as of the recent fund facts signals a solid entry point.

The key downside scenario here is a ceasefire that doesn't hold. If the Iran conflict re-escalates and the Hormuz chokepoint remains disrupted, European and Asian industrial stocks will continue to underperform, and AVIV's recovery thesis will stall. I'd also keep an eye on the CUSMA negotiations in July—Canada is a material part of the portfolio, and any re-escalation in U.S.-Canada trade tensions would add another layer of headwind on top of the Iran situation.

Bottom Line

AVIV is a well-constructed, actively managed international large-cap value ETF that has earned its 0.25% fee by tracking its benchmark (and beating it recently) closely while holding a differentiated portfolio skewed toward industrial, materials, and energy names that have genuine near-term tailwinds if the ceasefire holds. AVIV makes a coherent case—it's a focused value tilt on developed markets, run by a capable team, and available for less than the cost of a passive EAFE value alternative.

The war-driven sell-off has brought the fund down into the $70s and created what I think is an attractive entry point. I rate AVIV a buy on the ceasefire, with the clear caveat that this is not the fund to hold if the conflict re-escalates. For investors with a 12-to-18-month horizon who believe the worst of the Iran shock is behind us, AVIV is one of the more decent risk-reward ETFs in the international equity space right now.

data.title: Foundayo Explained: Lilly's New Weight Loss Pill And The Amazon Effect
data.description:
Thesis

The FDA has just approved Eli Lilly’s (LLY) latest GLP-1 therapy, Foundayo. It’s tipped to come with a number of advantages to compete on, including price, ease of use, and accessibility, as I'll explain. So, not just innovation alone. Foundayo here would reinforce my buy-case thesis in that it meaningfully lowers barriers to entry via oral dosing, simplified administration, competitive pricing, and it is now set to scale distribution via Amazon (AMZN). I think the Amazon factor is going to be a big deal moving forward, and to me, this is how Lilly will widen their addressable market. It's more about moving into a much larger, more convenience-driven population here, which is where the real volume upside is. In my last coverage, I went over Lilly's latest financials and how they came in such contrast to the competition, and now with the latest commercial moves for Foundayo, I think we could see that gap widening.

Foundayo, how it works

As you know, Lilly has just announced that their newly approved drug Foundayo/orforglipron is available for sale in the U.S. Now, I see this as a very big shift for the weight loss market for a number of different reasons. Unlike the earlier GLP-1–based treatments, Foundayo is a once-daily oral pill. It was approved by the U.S. FDA on April 1st of this year, for adults with obesity, or those who are overweight with weight-related conditions. As per the directions, it is to be used alongside diet and exercise. It can be taken at any time throughout the day, with or without food or water, but the tablets must be swallowed whole. So as far as therapies go, this couldn’t be easier for the patient.

If we look into the specifics, there are a number of available strengths ranging from 0.8 mg up to about 17.2 mg. That’s to allow for gradual dose escalation depending on tolerability and response. That range is simply there as a dose-escalation strategy, which is pretty standard for GLP-1–based therapies. We typically see that patients get started on a lower dose at about 0.8 mg or 2.5 mg, and then they gradually increase over weeks or months. This should help the body adapt to the drug’s effects, especially the strain on the gastrointestinal system, to reduce the likelihood of side effects.

Mechanistically speaking, orforglipron is again a small-molecule, non-peptide GLP-1 receptor agonist. It mimics the action of the natural incretin hormone GLP-1 by enhancing a lot of satiety signals in the brain. This slows gastric emptying and improves metabolic regulation. The main goal here is to reduce calorie intake and lose weight. We saw previously that in Phase 3 trials such as the ATTAIN program, patients on the highest dose lost about 12.4% of body weight, or about 27 pounds, over a 72-week period. There were also improvements in cardiovascular risk markers like blood pressure and lipids, which was good to see.

Differentiation among GLP-1s

Now, every time there is a new weight loss pill on the market or in the works, I always ask what truly makes this one different. I see the weight loss industry as one of the fastest-moving markets, simply because the money and demand are there. Players are always trying new things to stay ahead of the game, and because there are so many ways to compete in this market, be it side effects, overall weight loss, price or ease of use, it leads to interesting dynamics. Each player really focuses on what they’re good at, and for Lilly and Foundayo, it’s not just that it’s oral, but rather how convenient it is compared to other oral GLP-1 attempts.

We know that earlier oral GLP-1 drugs, like oral semaglutide, require a pretty strict fasting and timing rules. However, Foundayo can be taken without any food or water restrictions. It’s a major adherence barrier. Let's take a quick example here with Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) Rybelsus. A patient, a lot of the time it will be someone with type 2 diabetes or obesity, has to take it first thing in the morning on an empty stomach, with only a small sip of water, really no more than about 120 mL. They then have to wait at least 30 minutes before eating, drinking anything else, or, importantly, taking any other medications. Now, that might sound manageable on paper, but in practice, it’s quite restrictive, especially for the very population most likely to use it here. Those people with obesity/diabetes are already struggling with structured eating patterns. They usually wake up hungry, want coffee immediately, or need to take multiple morning medications, which is very common in diabetes.

Pricing

Now, it is still very early days, and pricing can, of course, change and will most likely differ from patient to patient, but from an access and pricing standpoint, Lilly is clearly setting up Foundayo here for broader uptake. What they want is accessibility. They want people not taking a weight loss pill, who have heard about them and are considering taking them, to have the easiest path possible to starting. That also comes with discretion, in that a large portion of those on GLP-1s don’t actually tell others they are on a plan. Now, in the previous days when injectables were costing in the hundreds per month, this was rather difficult. Many needed payer support, which then created a barrier to use in that they would have to go through the clinical process.

As for Foundayo, we’ve heard from Lilly that some insured patients may pay as little as $25/month, whilst self-pay pricing starts at about $149/month. The big thing here is that Lilly wants to undercut many injectable competitors that often exceed $1,000/month without coverage. Another way to ease the pathway into taking GLP-1s is to offer home delivery. This is a bigger factor than most think. Again, it allows for discretion, which many patients would prefer, which is why it is being distributed through LillyDirect with home delivery. But as I said, big pharma GLP-1 manufacturers have lots of ways to compete and will stick to what they’re good at. However, distribution and customer access have always been a challenge, which is why we’re seeing so many pharma companies turn to telehealth providers in recent times. Telehealth companies have a massive advantage simply from data accumulation. If we take the most predominant one recently in Hims & Hers (HIMS), their subscriber base is extremely valuable. They already know who would be most open to a weight loss pill based on customer data, and those already on one treatment subscription are far more open to taking a second. Again, this comes down to ease of access. This is why we saw such a big reaction to Novo re-signing that partnership with Hims; it’s a huge access advantage that big pharma wants.

Ease of access: the Amazon factor

So Novo has Hims, and now we’ve just heard that Lilly is set to offer Foundayo via Amazon. I see this as almost as big as a telehealth partnership. You see, the Amazon partnership is really about removing as much friction as they can from every step of the patient journey; they’re not just lowering prices here. The only advantage I see a telehealth company having over this is a more concentrated customer base. If we think of traditional pathways, getting a GLP-1 medication would involve quite a few steps. You’d have to book a doctor’s appointment, get a prescription, then find a pharmacy that has the drug in stock, which used to be a real issue with GLP-1 shortages. You’d wait in line and sometimes deal with insurance approvals.

What Amazon is essentially doing for Lilly here is compressing that entire chain into something a lot closer to a consumer e-commerce experience. The customer base they’re accessing here is any average person with an Amazon account, which is massive. I see the process working as something like this. You would have to get a prescription; this is, of course, still required and essential. A patient either visits their own doctor or uses a telehealth service, and keep in mind this could be Amazon’s One Medical, if they’re enrolled. So I expect Amazon to update the One Medical portal to have more prominent Foundayo advertising in the near term.

As for prescription routing, instead of sending the prescription to a local pharmacy, it’s sent directly to Amazon Pharmacy. You would then check out like a normal purchase on your Amazon account, see the medication, confirm insurance if applicable or pay the cash price. As for delivery, a lot of the time the medication can arrive the same day, similar to ordering your household items. In some One Medical clinics, I know that patients can even pick it up immediately with the in-office kiosks right after their appointment. It’s a major shift, and I see it as Lilly wanting to turn the medical process into something that feels more like ordering groceries.

Valuation risk

As for Lilly’s valuation, I think it would be reflective of a company which the market sees as a long-duration growth story. If we look at the headline multiples, the stock screens as pretty expensive. It trades at around 27x to 28x forward earnings and about 10.5x forward sales, both of which are well above healthcare sector medians. The premium would look even more pronounced on an enterprise basis, with EV to EBITDA sitting at about 22x forward, nearly double the sector norm. This tells me investors are not really valuing Lilly on current earnings alone here, but more so on the expected expansion of its obesity and diabetes franchise, particularly with drugs like tirzepatide and now Foundayo. Essentially, I see the market pricing in sustained high revenue growth, some strong pricing power and long-term dominance in the GLP-1 category with the current developments. I see this reshaping the company’s revenue mix over the next decade.

Now, that growth expectation is reflected more favourably in the PEG ratio here, which adjusts valuation for earnings expansion. Lilly’s forward PEG is about 1.2x and trailing PEG at 0.4x, which would imply that, relative to their projected EPS growth of about 40% in 2026 and tapering thereafter, the stock is not as stretched as those raw price-to-earnings multiples would imply. In other words, I feel Lilly looks expensive in absolute terms, but appears more reasonably valued on a growth-adjusted basis if we assume those growth forecasts materialise. The key risk in this valuation is execution. That current price would suggest GLP-1 adoption is going to continue scaling globally, with improving access and new formulations to expand the addressable market. Any slowdown in uptake going forward could challenge that premium.

Looking ahead

So just to sum up, Foundayo, for me, would represent more than just another entrant in the GLP-1 space here. It rather signals a shift toward mainstream, scalable obesity treatment. Lilly wants to combine oral convenience, flexible dosing, competitive pricing, and an increasingly frictionless process via common platforms like Amazon. What they’re doing is addressing the biggest barriers that we’ve seen historically limiting uptake. Yes, execution risks remain, and the valuation is already a big chunk of high expectations, but the broader direction is clear: the market is moving toward a much simpler, more accessible therapy plan. And Lilly is positioning itself at the centre of that transition.

data.title: Sabesp: A New Privatization As An Opportunity!
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on Sabesp shares (SBS). This article is part of my initial coverage thesis, and my last article about the company was published on August 15, 2025.

My intention with this article is to analyze the feasibility of Sabesp's participation in the privatization of Copasa, and how this would make Sabesp's investment thesis even more attractive.

Sabesp

SBS is the largest company in the sanitation sector in Brazil and one of the largest in the world. The company is responsible for supplying treated water in 375 municipalities in the Brazilian state of São Paulo, including the metropolitan region, serving more than 28 million people with water and almost 25 million with sewage collection. The company was controlled by the state government of SP from its foundation until July 2024, when it was privatized via share offering, becoming a corporation, without defined control.

Revisiting the thesis

I was skeptical about Sabesp's thesis in 2024; even the title of my first article was, Privatization Is Already Priced In. However, I observed the following results, and it was incredible how the executives showed the ability to cut costs and increase efficiency.

On March 26, 2025, I raised the recommendation from hold to buy, and mentioned how the company was becoming more agile after privatization. Little has changed since then, but one event has the potential to be a major milestone in Sabesp's history: participation in the privatization of Copasa. Sabesp has interest in the asset, but before I show the numbers, let's take a brief overview of Copasa.

The target

Founded in the 1960s, Copasa defines and executes the basic sanitation policies of the State of Minas Gerais, providing water supply, sewage, and solid waste services. The company provides water services to more than 600 municipalities and sewage services in more than 300 municipalities.

Copasa went public on the Brazilian stock exchange, B3 (BOLSY) in 2006 and is controlled by the Brazilian state of Minas Gerais, with 50.03% of the capital. Romeu Zema was elected to the state government in 2018, and with his liberal stance, he defined that the privatization of state-owned companies would be the strategy to solve fiscal problems. The privatization process has been slow since then, but the company's privatization may finally come to fruition.

Privatization Model

In January of this year, the Government of Minas Gerais defined the privatization model. The model adopted will be a public offering of secondary distribution of shares. Therefore. There will be no issuance of new shares, but rather the sale of existing shares. In this format, the capital raised goes to the state's coffers, and not to the company's cash flow. The objective is to resolve the state’s serious fiscal problem.

The details indicate that the government's idea is to find an investor who owns 30% of Copasa shares and is prohibited from selling any percentage of their shareholding for four years. After this period, the strategic investor will be able to transfer up to 50% of the shares, as long as it meets the goals of universalizing the provision of water supply and sewage services in all municipalities served by Copasa. Now, let's move on to the financial details of the possible deal.

My Opinion About The Possible Deal

There is no official announcement about the deal, so I will create a hypothetical scenario. The table below consolidates the main financial information of the two companies. My objective with the comparison is to show the synergies between the companies.

Sabesp has a market cap five times larger than Copasa and also a lower net debt/EBITDA ratio. The Government of Minas Gerais' requirement for investments in the context of the sanitation framework makes sense; after all, Sabesp's CapEx is much higher than Copasa's.

In my opinion, if Sabesp acquires the 30% stake at the current enterprise value (I consider it a reasonable multiple), the company will have to pay $1.7 billion. This would increase Sabesp's leverage from 1.9x EBITDA to 2.6x EBITDA. If Sabesp makes this investment, this could be a "second act" in the new history of Sabesp (after privatization), as I will explain in the next chapter.

Valuation

Sabesp reached the highest EV/EBITDA multiple in its history. According to SA tools, in the last 12 months the company's EV/EBITDA was 10.62x (49% above its 5Y average). This shows how effective management has been since the company's privatization in mid-2024

As efficiency gains become tighter, participation as a partner in Copasa could be a new trigger for the thesis. If the company becomes a partner and carries out a similar turnaround at Copasa, why not imagine that Sabesp could reach the multiples of its global competitors that trade at 13.5x EBITDA?

Sabesp is the company best positioned to become Copasa's partner, and if my thesis materializes again and Sabesp reaches the valuation of its global peers (13.5x EBITDA), this implies an upside of 27%. Therefore, I reiterate the recommendation to buy Sabesp shares, and I am confident with the recommendation since SA's powerful Quant tools indicate a strong purchase.

Investor Day

Copasa could be great for Sabesp, but the thesis has other triggers. The Investor Day recently took place (09 April), and many insights were shared. In my opinion, the main insight was the new growth strategy.

Management stated that the company is evaluating the possibilities in new services, expansion beyond the state of São Paulo, operations in other regions, and even abroad.

In this context, the market should closely monitor discussions about the Universaliza program, whose launch is expected by the end of 2026. The company also mentioned the possibility of adding new infrastructure segments to the portfolio in the future.

Latest earnings results

The company's results remain excellent, and Q4 once again showed gains in efficiency, reducing costs, expenses, and provisions. Costs and expenses fell 10.6% YoY, which allowed a strong increase of 13% YoY in EBITDA and EBITDA margin (+880 bps YoY to 59.6%). The company is expected to report its next results on May 7th.

But I will ensure an unbiased analysis, and as the reader can see above, the company reported revenue and EPS below the expectation. As I said, the company is making huge investments in the context of the Legal Framework for Sanitation.

This presents a big impact on CapEx and also on the financial result. As we know, the worse the financial result, the worse the net income. Therefore, Sabesp can see a pressure on the net income until 2030 due to investments.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Although the deal is promising, Sabesp's management is very cautious with the business. Executives indicate that sanitation regulation in Minas is not as robust as the São Paulo model, which could bring greater risks to the business.

At the same time, the quick deadline doesn’t help either. Hydrological risk is another important factor; after all, companies in the region often deal with increased pumping and treatment costs, which can be another impediment to the deal.

Finally, Sabesp doesn't just have the privatization of Copasa on the table. The company recently acquired EMAE, and the Universaliza São Paulo program bids are viewed favorably due to the even clearer synergies. These tenders should take place in the second half of the year.

The Bottom Line

My objective with this article was to describe in detail the opportunity that Sabesp has on the table. The state-owned company in the Brazilian state of Minas Gerais, called Copasa, is expected to be privatized in the coming weeks, and a deal with Sabesp has a high chance of occurring.

Management has done an exceptional job at Sabesp since its privatization in 2024. It is no surprise that the company's multiple jumped to the highest level in its history. But as efficiency gains become smaller and smaller, this deal has the potential to be a new catalyst for Sabesp's thesis.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Sabesp shares. The company would not have financial difficulties in carrying out the deal, and this could generate a new re-rating, which would make the risk-return ratio even more attractive.

data.title: EchoStar: Potential Bull Trap At Play - Take Gains Off The Table
data.description:
I previously rated AST SpaceMobile (AST) as a Hold in April 2026, attributed to the premium valuations from the SpaceX IPO exuberance.

SATS Suffers An Elon Musk Premium

1. SpaceX Exuberance

Similar to ASTS, EchoStar (SATS) has also been a beneficiary of the ongoing space-related boom, buoyed by SpaceX's upcoming IPO valued at $1.75T.

These are important developments indeed, since SATS is a diversified connectivity company offering numerous services, including pay-TV, Wireless, and Broadband and Satellite Services, amongst others.

Particularly, thanks to:

  • the FCC resolution,
  • the consequent 3.45 GHz/600 MHz spectrum licenses sale for $23B to AT&T,
  • the AWS-4/H-block spectrum licenses sale for $19B to SpaceX ($8.5B cash, $8.5B in SpaceX stock, and $2B of cash interest payments), and
  • the unpaired AWS-3 licenses are sold for $2.6B to SpaceX (in stock),

it is apparent that SATS has executed an excellent renewal beyond the prior growth headwinds.

For reference, pending the completion of the two deals, SATS currently reports an inflated net debt of $22.9B in FY2025 (-8.7% YoY) and a net debt to OIBDA ratio of 15.36x (a year ago at 13x) against its telecom services peer group averages of 2.62x.

Once the deals go through, SATS may be able to rapidly deleverage their balance sheet, while gaining excellent exposure to SpaceX's upcoming IPO, thanks to the $8.5B of SpaceX stock value as of September 2025 and $2.6B as of November 2025.

Based on the more than doubling in SpaceX's valuation from 2025 levels of $800B to the current pre-IPO levels of $1.75B, SATS may very well be holding on to a "gold mine" worth up to ~$39B, based on:

  • the estimated ~2.8% stake in SpaceX, pre-merger with xAI,
  • the post-merger transaction value of $1.25T, which values SpaceX at $1T and xAI at $250B (SpaceX at 80% and xAI at 20%), and
  • the estimated SpaceX (xAI included) IPO valuation of $1.75T.

This development perhaps explains why SATS' Enterprise Value has risen dramatically from $34.52B in August 2025 (pre-license sale) to $65.17B at the time of writing, with much of the premium from the SpaceX equity already priced in.

This is especially since the SpaceX deal allows SATS' access to the former's next-generation Starlink Direct to Cell service, allowing them to directly compete with many other satellite-based connectivity companies.

2. Near-Term Bottom-Line Noise

Given the FCC resolution and the upcoming spectrum sale, I expect to see numerous bottom-line headwinds in the near term, arising from SATS' "abandonment and decommission process for certain portions of our 5G Network."

The latter has contributed to the hefty non-cash asset impairments of $17.63B in FY2025, with further impairments expected through FQ2'26, lending credibility to the management not offering any forward guidance in the recent earnings call.

At the same time, SATS' core offerings continue to face growth headwinds, particularly in:

  • the Pay-TV segment, with the ongoing cord cutting triggering their declining subscriber base by -10% YoY in FQ4'25, the consequently deteriorating topline performance by -11.6% YoY, and the adj OIBDA by -14.5% YoY,
  • the Wireless segment, with subscriber growth of +7.4% YoY in FQ4'25, albeit with the intensifying competition with other legacy players triggering the flattish performance on a QoQ basis and the stagnant ARPUs on a QoQ/YoY basis, and
  • the Broadband and Satellite Service, with the intensifying competition with other satellite-based players triggering their declining subscribers by -16.3% YoY and backlog by -12.5% YoY.

While the management has signaled their "close to a breakeven business" in the Wireless segment, it is apparent that the next two quarters are likely to suffer bottom-line noises before eventually normalizing in H2'26, after the completion of their multi-quarter decommissioning work and the supposed SpaceX IPO sometime in June 2026.

As a result, while SATS has yet to offer any forward guidance, their FY2025 revenue performance at $15B (-5.2% YoY) and FY2025 OIBDA at $1.49B (-8% YoY) do not inspire confidence.

The same has been embedded in the consensus forward estimates, albeit with a potentially narrowing adj. EPS losses are attributed to the reduced interest expenses assuming their eventual deleveraging.

3. Elon Musk Premium

On the one hand, SATS appears to be compellingly valued at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 4.35x as the second-largest satellite Internet Service Providers [ISP] with a 22.2% market share, against:

  • the largest provider with 71.8% market share in Q2'25 - SpaceX (where Starlink is embedded) at an estimated EV/Sales valuation of 94.59x, based on the IPO estimates of $1.75T and the 2025 revenues of $18.5B, and
  • the third largest provider at 5.8% market share - Viasat, Inc. (VSAT) at 2.79x.

On the other hand, given the overly done +763% rally from the 52-week low, it is apparent that SATS remains expensive at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 32.03x against the FY2025 OIBDA margins of 9.9%.

A similar conclusion has also been derived after comparing it to its legacy connectivity peers, including,

  • Verizon (VZ) at 6.92x/36.1% in FY2025,
  • Comcast Corporation (CMCSA) at 5.87x/30.2% in FY2025 (diversified, including media/theme park),
  • Charter Communications (CHTR) at 5.74x/41.4% in FY2025,
  • T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS) at 9.02x/38.4% in FY2025, and
  • AT&T (T) at 7.22x/36.9% in FY2025, respectively,

with SATS appearing rather expensive here, as similarly observed against VSAT at 8.32x/33.6% over the LTM, pending the eventual improvement in SATS' adj. EBITDA margins closer to its peers.

These developments underscore that the SpaceX exuberance has been overdone, with SATS currently enjoying the "Elon Musk Premium" as similarly observed in Tesla (TSLA).

SATS Is Initiated As A Sell Here

For now, the exuberance surrounding space-related stocks has been well observed in SATS' near-vertical rally since mid-2025, albeit with the stock seemingly failing to break out of the $132 resistance levels and a double top bull trap potentially forming at current highs.

My conjecture is further supported by SATS' notably overbought technical indicators, with it remaining to be seen if the stock may be able to break out of the prior 52-week high of $132s to chart a new high.

I am rather skeptical, attributed to the premium already embedded in its valuations discussed above, worsened by the potential volatility from the elevated short interest ratio of 19.96% at the time of writing.

If anything, I am uncertain if SpaceX may be able to keep much of the premium after the much anticipated IPO, attributed to the potential profit-taking, as similarly observed in 2025, at which companies "that debuted in 2025, excluding closed-end funds and blank-check vehicles, climbed 13.9% on a weighted average basis, underperforming the S&P 500 Index’s 16% gain."

While I am of the opinion that SpaceX may continue to enjoy the "Elon Musk Premium," it is also no secret that TSLA has already lost -30% of its value since the 52-week highs while being rather volatile with a beta coefficient of 1.67x.

SATS' exposure to SpaceX implies that the former is likely to be similarly expensive and volatile moving forward, with the stock only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite.

Combined with the lackluster OIBDA margins, the uncertain path to GAAP profitability, the potential capital losses arising from the technical double top/bull trap at current levels, and the two-month gap to SpaceX's upcoming IPO in June 2026, I prefer to initiate a Sell rating for investors who have gotten in at the low double digits.

Other investors may want to wait for the steep correction to occur before initiating a position and/or dollar cost averaging.

data.title: Teladoc Health: Improving Fundamentals Support A Turnaround Story
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Teladoc Health (TDOC) is attempting to move away from an acquisition-based business model that has been cyclical and focused on marketing, toward a more stable and sustainable B2B model that utilizes data. Due to its customer acquisition-based model for the BetterHelp segment, the previous strategy was costly and created numerous problems that led to low lifetime customer value and a lack of profitability. Teladoc is currently changing how it operates by securing increased reimbursement from insurance companies, allowing it to convert temporary customers into permanent, high-value revenue streams.

Teladoc's integrated care segment now utilizes an AI-based Pulse platform that identifies potential risks associated with high-risk patients before they experience costly and serious adverse events. Another key component of this transition is the movement from reactive telemedicine to predictive care. By providing employers and health plans with tangible ROI, Teladoc will have the ability to charge premium prices and secure longer-term contract agreements. In addition, Teladoc has large longitudinal patient datasets which were obtained through the acquisition of Livongo and the development of its own device-driven database. This creates barriers to entry for competitors that are attempting to develop similar data advantages within the next several years.

From a financial perspective, Teladoc continues to improve stability and operations. FCF was $167 million in 2025, which is a big turnaround from negative FCF just a few years ago. In addition, the company has taken steps to strengthen its balance sheet, including paying down outstanding convertible debt, which has resulted in ~$781 million in cash and little near-term debt. Overall, Teladoc is in a much better position, where it can now fund operations and its transition internally. Margins have also seen improvement through a more favorable BetterHelp revenue mix and AI-powered workflow efficiencies. When combined with recent activist shareholder interest, I believe Teladoc's chances of a re-rating could improve if management executes successfully. Due to these factors, I'm rating Teladoc a Buy.

BetterHelp

The biggest challenge faced by Teladoc to date is BetterHelp. In FY 2025, BetterHelp's revenue was $950 million, which was a decline of 9% YoY. This is in line with recent trends, with the last four years showing flat or declining revenue. These challenges include high cost of user acquisition, high rates of "seasonal" churn, and lack of recurring revenue. The majority of individuals who found BetterHelp using paid search and/or social media utilized the service for a short period and cancelled. This created a continuous loop, depleting the marketing budget while producing very little new revenue.

In order to mitigate these issues, Teladoc purchased UpLift in April 2025 and gained the company's extensive connections to more than 100 million covered lives as well as a network of over 1,500 licensed therapists. More importantly, the purchase gave Teladoc a way to process insurance payments for the first time. Prior to this, BetterHelp had trouble scaling due to issues creating a successful payment structure. To date, early results validate a change in model. In 2025, BetterHelp generated about $13 million in insurance revenue with management expecting this to grow to $75-$90 million in 2026, representing a 6-7x increase from the previous year. This insurance revenue is expected to grow within Teladoc's current footprint in 20 states and nationwide before the end of the year. Unlike previous D2C customers who arrive at BetterHelp after paying for digital advertisements, insurance customers arrive from employers or insurers at significantly reduced customer acquisition costs. Furthermore, customers have a longer lifetime value as their therapy expenses are reimbursed by health plans.

One key difference for Teladoc is its access to UpLift's reimbursement processing capabilities, which took years for Teladoc to build itself. Additionally, through partnership agreements with organizations like AARP, Teladoc's combined platforms will have direct access to nearly 125 million household members. This provides the largest possible distribution channel available for the insurance-based model compared to all D2C competitors.

PulseAI

Teladoc's integrated care segment, which focuses on serving B2B clients, has often had the highest number of virtual visits within the overall industry. With the release of Pulse, Teladoc now has the opportunity to provide an AI-based classification system which identifies patients with chronic conditions that may be showing signs of deterioration and thereby creates opportunities for costly acute-care episodes. This separates them from the traditional model and improves their value proposition. According to Teladoc CEO Chuck Divita in a recent investor presentation at JPMorgan,

Think about it as our data engine. We then can take not only our own data, which is extensive, but bring in other data sources and leverage AI against that to help identify trends and personalization.

Put simply, Teladoc’s Pulse product allows clinicians to use AI to determine if a patient is showing signs of deterioration in their chronic condition. If this were the case, proactive interventions would assist in managing the condition better. The company also has a data moat built from approximately two decades of historical data generated by Teladoc’s virtual clinical interactions with its customers, historical data generated by Livongo, and lab results as well as data obtained from remote monitoring devices. No competitor currently collects datasets like these at this scale, giving them a competitive advantage. Employers and health plans are willing to pay for these benefits since they create measurable returns for the employer/health plan client when Teladoc does identify a high-risk patient. This represents a completely new value proposition compared to simply providing access to telemedicine services, although, the addition of telemedicine services provides Teladoc with an opportunity to charge higher fees for these services. Since Teladoc is utilizing AI to reduce time spent on administrative functions, management expects integrated care segment margins to increase to 15-16% in 2026.

Activist Pressure

Recently, Pineal Capital sent an open letter to Teladoc's board of directors, suggesting that Teladoc should reduce costs, execute a $200 million stock repurchase program, and possibly separate BetterHelp from Integrated Care (so investors can take advantage of the "sum-of-the-parts" value that is being suppressed by having these two businesses together). With integrated care expected to continue to grow at its high margin rate as a B2B enterprise health platform, this strategy could be justified.

I believe these actions could lead to a potential re-rating of Teladoc's valuation, however, this depends on management's ability to execute and reduce costs, improve capital allocation, or to pursue strategic separation. This could improve investor perception and lead to higher multiples that are closer to peers.

Valuation and Improving Financials

Teladoc has historically had trouble with profitability, even with recent improvements. One of these issues is BetterHelp, one of the companies largest revenue contributors, which has generated lower margins because of high customer acquisition costs and high customer turnover. This forces Teladoc to continue investing in marketing to support BetterHelp, which limits profits even when the business is growing.

On the other hand, Teladoc's integrated care segment has stronger margins as a B2B model, which has helped offset the drag in BetterHelp's D2C model. However, Teladoc is making steps to reduce this drag by growing its insurance-reimbursement based models and AI-driven systems. Although the company still faces some of this drag, the transition seems to be taking shape as results continue to improve from previous years.

Teladoc generated $167 million in FCF in 2025, marking a strong turnaround from negative levels in prior years. It used this money to pay off its outstanding convertible notes and to lower its debt. Due to the lower near-term debt levels, Teladoc now has approximately $781 million in cash, and its short-term debt obligations have been significantly lowered.

For 2026, Teladoc is expecting revenue of $2.47-2.59 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $266-308 million. This would represent an approximate 11% margin on revenue at the mid-point. While modest, the progression of EBITDA from negative three years ago to over $200M today demonstrates the business' earnings potential. Management expects that BetterHelp will generate adjusted EBITDA margins of 3-4.6% as they continue to invest in it, which is similar to the 4.4% adjusted EBITDA margin in 2025.

The difference in the valuation multiples of Teladoc relative to its peer group is relatively large. With pure D2C peers like Hims & Hers trading at ~1.7x forward revenue and pure B2B Amwell (which has plummeting sales) trading at ~0.46x, Teladoc's multiples appear unfairly low. Based on a ~0.38x forward revenue multiple, the market cap at approximately $940 million represents less than 5x adjusted EBITDA based on current operations. Additionally, the market should assign some credit to increased revenue and higher margins associated with the growth of BetterHelp's insurance revenue base and Pulse's margin contributions. The situation has continued to improve for Teladoc, yet the multiples are still depressed. This ultimately gives a favorable risk/reward at current levels and as a result, I'm rating Teladoc a Buy.

Risks

The most significant and uncertain assumptions are BetterHelp's increased revenue from its insurance business. Expansion into all 50 states will require solid execution and if any problems are incurred it could hurt management's revenue guidance for the segment.

There continues to be a high level of competitive activity in D2C mental health services with companies such as Cerebral and Talkspace (TALK), which continue to advertise heavily for their services. A rapid decline in D2C base customers due to competition could lead guidance misses.

Conclusion

Teladoc Health is now positioned as a next-gen healthcare platform, not just a traditional telehealth provider. With the transition to an insurance-based revenue model, and the increased scope of its B2B integrated care segment, Teladoc is creating a foundation for predictability of revenue growth and sustainable margin improvement. Overall, this leaves Teladoc with a structurally stronger business than what is reflected in the price of the stock.

Another key factor for the new Teladoc is PulseAI, which expands the value proposition of the company. Through the use of Teladoc's large data set to proactively identify and manage high-risk patients, Teladoc is moving along the healthcare value chain (from access to care to actual improved health outcomes and reduced costs). These improvements enhance client retention, support premium pricing, and capture long-term contracts.

In terms of the financial aspects of the company, it is shifting into a new phase of operational leverage. Teladoc has positive FCF, a strengthened balance sheet, and increasing EBITDA margins which indicates that the company is transitioning to an efficient and scalable profitability model. The combination of this, along with the undervaluation of the company when compared to peers, creates an opportunity where even modest execution could drive a substantial increase in valuation. As a result of the growing fundamentals of Teladoc's business, I believe the company could see a re-rating over time if management continues to successfully execute, which is why I am rating Teladoc a Buy at current levels.

data.title: Two 12%+ Yielding BDC Bargains (One Is My Top Deep-Value Pick)
data.description:
Private credit and especially direct lending which is where most BDCs (BIZD) operate is probably one of the last places where durable income investors might be searching opportunities.

If the requirement is to a) not lose money and b) lock in predictable and sustainable (e.g., not funded by NAV) dividends then BDCs might indeed seem speculative.

Based on what we can see in the headlines and on the ground with practical examples such a stance is only logical.

There are many unknowns in the air where each of them can potentially result in notable capital destruction.

SaaS-related defaults, private BDC liquidity outflows, questionable process of fair value marks and potential downturn in the overall credit cycle are just some examples that might trigger bad consequences.

And it is not that the bears have no practical "case studies" to cling to. In fact, they have the "luxury" to pinpoint to, say, two well-established BDCs (with strong brand names) that have recently lost about one third of their market cap levels due to exploding non-accruals.

See the YTD price performance chart of FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) and BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC) below:

Now, as I have articulated in my previous articles the BDC land is not as even or similar as it might seem. The differences among managers, strategies and capital allocation tactics can be huge. It is something similar to REITs (VNQ) where it would be incorrect to equate data centers with office REITs, or hotels with industrial REITs.

Just take a look at my Top 3 BDC performance compared to that of the broader BDC market:

This shows that it is possible to insert BDCs into a portfolio without negatively diluting overall income and value durability (as long as the focus is put on high-quality picks).

Yet this kind of approach has two sides:

  1. It brings in a decent BDC yield component without aggressive risks.
  2. It comes with a reduced upside potential which is logical given that there has not been an evident repricing in this particular area (in top quality BDC segment).

Based on my research, I see two huge externally managed BDC bargains which over the past couple of months have plunged into rather deep discount territory.

And what makes the story extra compelling is the presence of robust fundamentals which are strong enough to fulfill the two main conditions for durable income investing: 1) income stability/sustainability and 2) NAV durability.

#1: MSDL, 12.6%

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) is a typical externally managed BDC which focuses on core middle market lending. Just as almost all of its peers, MSDL has been subject to quite drastic repricing:

Currently, MSDL trades at a P/NAV of 0.71x which is slightly below the sector average of 0.74x. The discount is significant but what is also important is that MSDL trades below the sector average.

In my view, MSDL has the key fundamentals in a stronger position than for its peers or an average BDC out there.

So, the investment case really has three drivers:

  1. Potential upside from the sector-wide recovery.
  2. Potential upside from MSDL's becoming priced in line with the sector (or very likely trading at a premium to the peers).
  3. Attractive and protected dividend income.

As of Q4 2025, MSDL's portfolio looked like this:

We can see that the portfolio is very diversified across different borrowers and sectors. Granted, the software exposure stands out - just as for most externally managed BDCs - but the quality of underlying fundamentals clearly de-risk the situation.

Here are the most important considerations:

  • 96.2% of MSDL's loan book is tied to first-lien loans which are the safest in the BDC (direct lending) game. The sector average is 77.7%.
  • PIK income accounts for less than 4% of the total income, while the sector average is 8.5%. The PIK for MSDL has remained at these levels in all of the 2025 financial quarters (i.e., it is not a one-off that we see here).
  • The average portfolio company loan-to-value ratio is 40% which indicates that MSDL has not ventured into aggressively leveraged transactions.
  • The non-accrual rate for MSDL is 1.6% which is ~2x below the sector average of 3.5%.

Also, if we look at the recent unrealized losses, MSDL has delivered quite solid statistics. For instance, while in Q4 MSDL did indeed recognize markdowns the consequences to NAV were immaterial - i.e., these markdowns accounted for a bit less than 0.05% of the total NAV.

The NII per share generation (or core earnings) front is rock-solid as well. In Q4, MSDL reported $0.49 per share in NII which was ~$0.01 per share lower than in the previous quarter (mostly driven by lower SOFR headwinds).

Given the recent oil price dynamics and rising inflationary risks, it seems that new base rate cuts are off the table for 2026. So, I wouldn't' expect additional pressure from this aspect.

Moreover, the NII per share definitely benefits from well-structured debt funding portfolio where the lion's share of borrowings is attached to floating rates and the remaining part of fixed rate notes carry somewhat market-aligned yield levels (and the next refinancing event is in Q1 2027).

Finally, it is worth mentioning that recently MSDL revised its dividend downwards due to the pressures that came from lower base rate which compressed the ROE levels. The quarterly base dividend dropped from $0.50 per share to $0.45 per share which still offers an enticing yield of 12.6%.

Because of the realized cut, MSDL's base dividend coverage level has increased to ~109% which is again meaningfully above sector average of 97%. From this level MSDL can retain some part of NII which could be then used for growth funding in a rising spread environment (a timely moment considering how many private BDCs are pulling back their lending activities to handle the liquidity needs).

In a nutshell, MSDL is durable income producer which has a decent margin of safety that comes from three areas: 1) high-quality portfolio (e.g., conservative LTVs, first-lien), 2) surplus dividend coverage and 3) significant discount which sets a floor for the potential downside while providing a notable upside potential from the potential recovery and convergence to sector peer P/NAV levels.

#2: OTF, 12.5%

Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF) is for me probably a higher conviction play than MSDL. It has much deeper discount and even higher margin of safety on the fundamental side.

As of today, OTF's P/NAV stands at 0.63x.

This kind of P/NAV is similar to Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) and PennantPark Investment Corporation (PNNT). The former has registered massive non-accruals and is down ~42% on a TTM basis. The latter has one of the highest leverage levels in the sector and a huge reliance on joint venture income which is where the debt levels are even bigger than what would be allowed per BDC regulation (if these loan investments were on PNNT's balance sheet then the BDC 2:1 leverage ceiling would be breached). PNNT has lost ~30% of its market cap in the past year.

There are multiple reasons behind OTF's deep-value P/NAV:

  • Overall sector headwinds.
  • Ties to Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) which is OTF's asset manager and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) which is OTF's sister BDC (both have become subject to notable selloff).
  • Concentration in SaaS.
  • Gradual release of post IPO lock-up shares which flood the market with new supply of shares in a moment when the buying (demand) activity is depressed.

Here is how OTF has performed (price-wise) since its IPO:

However, if we peel back the onion a bit, we will notice how fundamentally sound OTF is and why we could with a reasonable degree of confidence assume that both the dividend and NAV will remain stable going forward.

From the points (negative share price drivers) that I mentioned above, the SaaS or software concentration aspect is the only one which might have some unfavorable impact on the NAV.

This is because of, as the name also suggest, above-average exposure to software - i.e., roughly 70% of OTF's portfolio is linked to software investments.

A couple of things.

First, the data below show that OTF's portfolio investments are spread across different sectors and, importantly, that the underlying portfolio companies are well-established with strong financial metrics. For example, the first-lien loan average EBITDA is $290 million which in combination with average LTV of 34% does not indicate elevated financial risk, to say the least.

Second, the portfolio quality has been almost excellent. In Q4 2025 the non-accrual statistic came in at 0.2% which is one of the lowest levels in the entire BDC space (not only public, but also private).

In fact, in Q4 OTF was one of the few BDCs which reported a net unrealized gain of the portfolio holdings which contributed to a 0.18 per share of NAV appreciation.

Third, OTF carries several investments at the company equity level. One of such investments is associated with Space Exploration Technologies or SpaceX. So, it means that if SpaceX's IPO will be successful then OTF would be in a position to harvest sizeable equity gains that will later feed into NAV growth, strengthening OTF's capacity to fund future growth.

Fourth, speaking of growth funding I have to say that OTF is arguably in one of the best (most comfortable) places relative to other peers. As of today, OTF has the fifth lowest leverage level in the system - i.e., the debt to equity is 0.75x.

Given the usual target capital structure of 1.20x, OTF has a long way to go which is obviously positive and accommodative to NII per share growth.

It must be noted that OTF does not have support from the retained NII as it is for MSDL. Based on Q4 data the dividend coverage was 86% which means that OTF has to utilize debt to close the gap.

However, this is fully logical given the overcapitalized balance sheet. Once OTF will lever the balance sheet up to ~1.20x, the base dividend should be well-covered (as it is also communicated by the management).

And here I'm not factoring in the potential boost from SpaceX IPO nor any tailwinds that might stem from a heightened spread environment (remember, to be a buyer in the current situation when most BDCs are dialing back is a clear opportunity and luxury).

In a nutshell, OTF offers a deep-value investment play which comes with durable dividends, robust fundamentals (high but de-risked SaaS exposure) and gigantic discount.

In my humble opinion, OTF is the best risk-reward case now (among externally managed BDCs and for those who can stomach price volatility).

data.title: TechnipFMC: We Prefer Saipem With More Room To Improve
data.description:
TechnipFMC plc (FTI) has been delivering nicely, with solid margins in both onshore and offshore development businesses. The margin improvements reflect a deliberate profit-first strategy in bidding for projects, as discussed in our previous coverage, that reflects a new phase of margin targets as compared to some of the still liquidating "legacy" backlog. But the thing is that having proven out marginality in these businesses, Saipem (SAPMF), soon to be merged with Subsea 7 (SUBCY), has more space to gain by improving margins, especially onshore. As it happens, Saipem still has an onshore backlog liquidating at bad economics that needs to be digested out. Saipem also trades at a much lower valuation than TechnipFMC despite being in really the same markets, with the industry being basically an oligopoly, particularly now with Saipem and Subsea 7 merging. TechnipFMC loses in the relative value argument. Then, while not the focus of the thesis, we'd also say the run-up in all these stocks' prices may be premature, even though the Iran War has upset oil markets and increased oil prices.

Results

Backlog growth was pretty significant, up 15%. Most of it is, of course, in the much larger subsea division, as opposed to onshore oil engineering and procurement projects. Total revenue was up 9% from last year on backlog growth and execution; sequentially, revenues were seasonally under pressure due to maintenance scheduled in the EoY period.

For the FY, subsea saw 11% growth in adjusted EBITDA, ignoring restructuring expenses, with margins coming up almost above 20%, with the medium-term target being around 21%. The quarterly margin was lower due to seasonality and reflects YoY deceleration as well, though not in terms of profits, with the profit discipline clearly bearing fruit. Continued achievement of EBITDA lift is expected as a result of more new-phase project bidding, where now the company has a more integrated approach and is more disciplined in raising margins and offering clearer delivery timelines, apparently also spawning more greenfield efforts with customers reconsidering previously ignored reserves.

There was less going on onshore, it would seem due to a slower NA market, but profitability is rising along with the subsea segment on better project quoting discipline.

In terms of backlog phasing, it is burgeoning with projects that are expected to see 2026 completion, especially in surface but also significantly in subsea. It is a general rule that later stages of these projects tend to liquidate at higher margins, since the delivery at these later stages takes advantage of infrastructural work, like preparing ports for pipe-laying vessels and things like that, such that incremental costs fall thereafter. This should be a help in raising margins to target, in addition to the fact that new backlog is supposed to be margin accretive.

Bottom Line

But when we note that the surface and subsea margins are really quite similar, and that competing Saipem EBITDA margins for onshore are in the low single digits, we just think that the improvement in the latter is inevitable as it digests out poor backlog, while there is less of a margin of safety in achieving further profit margin growth for FTI, reinforced by the fact that the margins are already reaching near-term targets. The legacy business is being divested out of Saipem. Otherwise, the offshore margins are more similar to each other with FTI, albeit higher. But FTI trades at a 14x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis, while Saipem trades at below 5x. FCFY is also considerably higher at Saipem than at FTI (11% forward vs. 4.62%). Both are beneficiaries of efforts to standardise delivery. Saipem is also likely to benefit from synergies from the Subsea 7 merger, particularly in vessel and other hard asset route economics with the greater density of projects that the combined company will be working on. We think there are more factors that are pushing Saipem up in terms of profitability.

In terms of the war, it has been a reason for a run-up in the stocks of oil services players, but it was delayed until very late March as opposed to the onset of the War.

While ceasefire talks are underway in Islamabad right now, prospects seem limited due to considerable demands from Iran, which nonetheless, through toll threats, puts a high floor on the oil price. That is our view as well. But the run-up in these stocks comes after a settling down of the oil futures curve, which is now forecasting effectively a normalization already by August-September 2026. We may not agree with this optimistic view, which would be to the benefit of oil services companies that will only get mandates if the price of oil seems to be staying sustainably higher, but we don't see the market action as coherent. Also, we aren't sure if these higher prices will prove sustainable enough to spur a step change in activity, even if we suspect that the oil futures curve may be too optimistic. Though factors such as possibly enduring Iranian control and enforcement at the Strait and a higher probability of renewed energy crises could be structural factors that do put upward pressure on energy prices. But opposite factors could be considerable unused OPEC+ supply still in store while phasing out supply cuts, and the geopolitical outcome possible that Iran backs off from the Strait. We don't want to try to predict what will happen.

As far as FTI is concerned, it's relatively overvalued, and there is more of an angle on Saipem.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: From Bye-Bye To Buy-Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
To those that have read my earlier work on Tesla (TSLA), I say that when the evidence points to a change of heart, it would be foolish not to listen and act. The evidence I refer to is multi-fold: recent developments pertaining to volume production of Cybercab, the re-taking of the #1 EV brand accolade from BYD, the removal of a key hurdle to Musk's compensation package, and the Model 2 SUV slated for next year.

Until now, I have lobbied hard against holding TSLA because all the data points led me to that belief, but now that Q1 2026 deliveries are known and the downward trajectory for the EV business has been established, it would be prudent to look at the value that TSLA stock now represents at the current price that is, ironically, 21 percent down from my last Strong Sell article.

At this price, investors can afford to look at TSLA as a buy, even as a speculative position and even without the strong conviction needed to justify such a view. The signs are emerging, and April could well be a pivotal month for the stock and the company. The past has validated my Strong Sell rating. Can the future equally validate my pivot to Buy? We shall soon see. In the meantime, I offer what I believe is a balanced view of the investment narrative around this controversially magnificent stock.

What I Am Bullish About Now?

I shall begin with the positives because there is still a lot going on that could drag the stock down further, but I need readers to focus on these positive aspects regardless. I acknowledge these negatives, some of which still persist, as being the lingering portion of my Strong Sell view until now. However, several new developments have come to light this month (and could come to light later on in the month) that force me to rethink my position.

#1: Giga Texas - The New Center of Tesla's AV Universe?

Musk appears to have kept the promise made in November 2025 to begin Cybercab mass production at Giga Texas. New information has just surfaced two days ago that shows more than 60 Cybercab-like vehicles (remnants of my skepticism, I will admit) were spotted in the "outbound lot" at the factory.

While the report also notes that these vehicles had steering wheels, which Musk originally said they would not (along with side mirrors and pedals), so one can assume these are not the final production units going to market, the fact that manufacturing has begun is, in itself, a major development that could potentially upend bearish narratives, including mine.

If all of this is indeed true - and I defer to the possibly biased but heretofore largely accurate reports from Teslerati contributors - then it implies that Giga Texas could eventually become the "epicenter of Tesla’s autonomous revolution". If this is true, and I believe it will eventually be, then moving its headquarters to the Lone Star State more than four years ahead of this development could, in a sense, be labeled as nothing short of prophetic.

The migration of key Tesla assets to Texas, including legal incorporation in 2024 and the breaking of ground on the Lithium refinery the year before, as well as Boring Company and SpaceX assets already located in Texas, an X unit supposedly beginning Texas operations, and other Texas-based news developments that include the latest one on Cybercab production - all of these are clues to Texas being Ground Zero for Tesla's new ambitions in the realm of robotaxis.

In terms of actual real-world performance, Tesla's L4 and L5 autonomy ambitions may not be as distant as I and many Tesla bears once thought. There are indications that Tesla will achieve a total of 10 billion autonomous miles driven by FSD deployed in its current on-road customer fleet by the middle of this year.

One should bear in mind that the 7 billion miles achieved are NOT unsupervised, and the first run of steering-sporting Cybercabs is proof that L4/5 autonomy might still be several months away from becoming reality, and even longer to become regulation-compliant in U.S. and international markets.

Having said that, it is equally true that the data from these billions of miles are invaluable to the training models to achieve full autonomy. This real-world data puts Tesla far ahead of Waymo, which has racked up "20+ million miles of real world driving and 20+ billion miles in simulation", per its website. One could argue that 20+ billion trumps 10 billion, but those are simulated miles, not real-world. On the other hand, 20+ million miles in real-world situations is about 20+ million miles more than Tesla has achieved, since all of those 7 billion miles done so far have been supervised, and across multiple levels of autonomy.

As things stand now, Cybercab production may be jumping ahead of L4/L5 capabilities, but we know that the hardware is likely already there. According to one report:

It appears that Tesla upgraded the Autopilot computer to a new component, which was first spotted in the parts catalogue on January 21. Soon after that, owners of the new Model Y checked and discovered that the Autopilot Hardware 4.5 computer (or AP45 as it is marked) has been in production since at least December 2025.

This could imply that HW5 is not too far away, despite Musk's usual ambitious target already being delayed by six months or more.

Putting it all together, it would appear that the pieces are gradually falling into place. The promise is slowly becoming reality, and this is essentially what the bulls are betting on with respect to Tesla's robotaxi ambitions. This is the ground floor, just as the Roadster and Model S trajectories were the bottom floors of its EV-led trajectory. The new trajectory will be AV-led, in my opinion.

#2: Current Valuation and Incoming Q1 2026 Earnings Report on April 22

My reason for the bullish pivot is not that TSLA is cheap now. Not by any measure. This year, valuations have dropped by a fifth, but they are still significantly higher than their five-year averages. So it is far from cheap.

Nevertheless, the 'Musk premium' still exists, and this has acted as a floor for the volatile stock, with technicals exhibiting a rising channel.

Even more relevant to my now-bullish thesis is that this premium will continue to exist for the foreseeable future, as will the floor support. And with the Cybercab catalyst now clearly in play, we could see significant gains as the stock bounces off yet another (and higher) support level.

The risk element here is that the stock may break the floor and head lower in the short term, because downward momentum is significant right now. This is also why Q1 2026 results could play a major role in testing that floor.

Analysts are looking for a top-line print of $22.75 billion, which represents a 17.6 percent year-on-year growth rate, and this is despite Q1 deliveries being a known component now. If one looks at the cadence of Street ratings over the past three years, one sees more bullishness weighing in on the stock price.

Indeed, over that period, the Street's consensus price targets have been moving higher, and the stock, although quite volatile around price target levels, seems to be keeping up.

As it stands, these same analysts now have a one-year PT showing 19 percent of potential upside, and I believe the stock will once again keep up despite the short-term risk that I mentioned just now.

#3: The Pivot to Affordability and Autonomy

Tesla seems to have realized that the competition is nipping at its heels in the EV space. Not two days ago, Reuters reported that a "Model 2" SUV pegged at $34,000 may be in the works for next year.

If this is true, it represents a potential catalyst, particularly since Musk signaled on the Q4 earnings call that Models S and X's "honorable discharge because we're really moving into a future that is based on autonomy"

we're seeing, obviously, the first steps along that way this year for Tesla, first major steps as we increase vehicle autonomy and begin to produce Optimus robots at scale.

This is quite possibly the most telling evidence that Tesla is, indeed, marching to a new drumbeat that could eventually rival its peak success in EV sales. Incidentally, Tesla is once again the #1 EV player after BYD sales tanked 25 percent in Q1. If a hard push to bring more affordable EVs to the market materializes, it could effectively stem the declines in production and deliveries.

The strategic shift to autonomy is also evident in how Tesla is aggressively investing in CapEx for Optimus. The winding down of Models S and X is part of a plan to produce up to 1 million Optimus robots a year using erstwhile production space for the outgoing EV models. As for the CapEx outlay, per CFO Taneja:

And at the moment, we are expecting that CapEx would be in excess of $20 billion. We'll be paying for 6 factories namely, the refinery, LFP factories, Cybercab, Semi, a new Megafactory, the Optimus factory. On top of it, we'll also be spending money for building our AI compute infrastructure, and we'll continue investing in our existing factories to build more capacity. And then also the related infrastructure along with it. And we'll also further expand our fleet of Robotaxi and Optimus. While this may seem a lot, we believe this is the right strategy to position the company for the next era, and we'll make such investments, as Elon mentioned, in a very capital-efficient manner.

The 2.5x CapEx jump from $8.5 billion in FY 2025 to "in excess of $20 billion" may not be fully covered by operating cash flows ($14.7 billion in FY 2025), but Tesla has ample short-term liquidity exceeding $44 billion that can fuel this ramp.

There will obviously be significant pressure from the declining bottom line over the past three years, but I see this level of CapEx as realistic over the next year or two, after which robotaxi and Optimus revenues should ramp up to start strengthening the bottom line and operating cash flows again.

In Conclusion

My shift from Strong Sell to Buy is obviously a major one, but it is based on the intensity of recent news flows, as well as key developments that have led to this point.

I do not presume to tell you that the stock is going to go up from here because I do see some short-term price weakness, but my thesis is a more long-term one that envelops Tesla's strategic shift from being an EV-heavy entity to one that is autonomy-heavy.

I think we are once again on the ground floor of an exciting journey ahead.

data.title: Western Midstream: A 9% Yield That Still Grows In A Downturn
data.description:
Western Midstream (WES) actively manages costs, capital intensity, and contract structures to ensure that its financials stay as agnostic to throughput variations as possible. Operating data demonstrates a very stable EBITDA range of ~$540-610m through periods of volatility in volumes and earnings. While that is not a sufficient reason for a buy on growth terms alone, that becomes a very strong anchor for income investors.

The combined returns from a ~9% yield and ~5% embedded EBITDA growth mean investors can look at early double-digit returns as a base case. And that through a valuation regime that has also remained within a defined band in the recent past. The buy thesis on income grounds does not rely on further rerating and should be able to accommodate ~10% multiple compression - that is if Western Midstream tests the valuation lows seen in the past couple of years. The core buy call relies on Western Midstream's positioning as a relatively cycle-agnostic play - something that looks structurally supported, repeatable, and proven through weak environments.

EBITDA Stability

Since the core of the thesis is a relatively stable EBITDA, it is important to assess the degree of stability compared to the volatility in net income over the past 5 years (as shown in the chart below). In fact, in 2025, EBITDA almost defied a sharp drop in net income - that has often been achieved through a set of very actively managed levers.

The GAAP EBITDA numbers show a dip in Q4 2025 (charts above), but adjusted EBITDA was reported at ~$636m. If accounting noise in adjusted EBITDA were removed, due to ~$30m of negative revenue recognition adjustments in the quarter, the numbers would have been up sequentially by ~5%. This is demonstrated EBITDA resilience through a period of volume and net income pressures. Even in Q4, there were temporary throughput curtailments due to negative gas pricing at Waha (producers chose to shut volumes rather than sell at uneconomic prices).

The guidance for 2026 is an EBITDA of ~$2.5-$2.7b, aiming for a ~5% EBITDA growth off a normalized base. This is in line with management's aim of long term average EBITDA growth in the mid-to-low single digits, despite an outlook for flat gas volumes and oil/NGL volumes down by low-to-mid single digits. That 2026 volume outlook may not change materially due to the Iran conflict and higher oil prices immediately, but it does improve Permian drilling economics (if the effects of the conflict are sustained). That means the environment becomes supportive for higher activity and associated gas over time. Budgets and infrastructure limits mean response is constrained and lagged, and importantly, it needs confirmation of a sustained higher energy price regime (and US strategic energy asset emphasis). I do not factor in any upside due to the energy prices variable (impact is indirect anyway), but I do think the crisis provides a factor that could reduce further volume contraction.

Western Midstream typically keeps EBITDA stability intact through measures that look structural and repeatable. Cost reductions are a lever that is actively used. The operation and maintenance expenses are down by ~12% YoY, through a series of optimizations that are expected to continue in 2026. That includes measures like fewer contractors, better sourcing, and facility redesign. These changes appear to be process improvements that should persist (not temporary cuts). Additionally, Western Midstream has cut capex by ~$200m (deferring low priority expansions). G&A expenses are also projected to stay flat despite integration activity (Aris) - balanced by optimization projects in IT systems, corporate efficiencies, etc. On the revenue side, protection due to fee-based and MVC structures means Western Midstream gets paid per unit or minimum volume regardless of actual throughput. All in all, EBITDA volatility is well managed and is expected to stay the same way, despite volume volatility.

Self-Funding Transition Mechanics

How does the stable EBITDA story translate to cash flow realities? The 2026 guided EBITDA of ~$2.6b should translate to ~$1.85-2.05b distributable cash flow (DCF) according to management estimates. Leaving aside ~$925m for capex (midpoint of the ~$0.85-$1b guidance), we still have ~$1.025b of post-capex cash generation - that is good internal funding capacity. Now, at ~$3.7 distribution per share for the ~393m shares outstanding, the distribution commitment adds up to ~$1.45b. That is a gap of ~$0.4-$0.45b against the post-capex cash, broadly using midpoint estimates (could change a bit depending on actual EBITDA, capex, etc.).

I am not worried about the earnings power because with ~$2.6b of EBITDA converting into ~$1.85-2.05b of DCF, the business is already generating significant cash before capital allocation decisions. The more relevant question is around capital intensity.

Current capex is limited in Pathfinder and North Loving II primarily, and these are already contracted and can be expected to contribute from 2027. So, over the course of a few quarters, we will see a double boost to the post-capex cash as capex rolls off and EBITDA expands. That gap of ~$0.4-$0.45b we are seeing today could be easily closed due to these effects, pulling Western Midstream into a self-funded regime. This transition is irrespective of how volumes move or commodity prices shift - this is a mechanical improvement from assets that are contracted and can be monetized.

As of today, too, that post-capex gap is not a cause of concern - I brought that up to show how post-capex cash is set to grow stronger, not to address issues in Western Midstream's buy thesis. Net debt today is ~$8b (with an EBITDA of ~$2.5-2.6b), implying leverage of ~3.1x, which is at the lower end of the sector. The strong balance sheet bridges the post-capex cash gap today seamlessly while enabling capex flexibility. Plus, Western Midstream has shown how it can use capex spending as a tool to stabilize capital allocation.

A Solid Income Play

The growth expectations with Western Midstream are as predictable as they can get, largely due to the company's emphasis on EBITDA stability. With an EBITDA growth of low to mid single digits and a yield of ~9% (with low single-digit distribution growth very much possible, given historical dividend growth trends).

Once Pathfinder and North Loving II are commissioned and start adding incremental fee-based volumes, EBITDA grows further while capex normalizes. Water segment expansion (Aris integration) increases exposure to produced water handling, which is structurally growing faster than hydrocarbons and is typically fee-based with long-term contracts. This adds a more stable and higher visibility revenue stream. Cost efficiencies are also lasting and structural.

Importantly, EBITDA and distribution stability are not dependent fully on volumes or higher drilling activity.
